sections.title: Der, Die, Das — Three Genders
sections.explanation: Every German noun has a grammatical gender: masculine (der), feminine (die), or neuter (das). This is called the Genus. Unlike English, gender is not always logical — 'das Mädchen' (the girl) is neuter! Gender must be memorized with each noun. The plural article is always 'die', regardless of gender. Always learn a noun together with its article: not just 'Tisch' but 'der Tisch'.
sections.tip: Always learn nouns with their article! Say 'der Tisch, die Lampe, das Buch' — never just 'Tisch, Lampe, Buch'.

exercises.prompt: What is the article for 'Tisch' (table)?
exercises.explanation: 'der Tisch' is masculine. Tables, chairs, and most objects ending in a consonant cluster tend to be masculine.

exercises.prompt: What is the article for 'Lampe' (lamp)?
exercises.explanation: 'die Lampe' is feminine. Most nouns ending in -e are feminine.

exercises.prompt: What is the article for 'Buch' (book)?
exercises.explanation: 'das Buch' is neuter. You must memorize this — there is no rule that helps here.

exercises.prompt: The plural article in German is always ___
exercises.hint: The same as the feminine singular article
exercises.explanation: In German, all plural nouns use the article 'die', regardless of their singular gender.

exercises.prompt: Why is 'das Mädchen' (the girl) neuter despite referring to a female?
options: Because girls are considered neuter in German culture
Because it ends in -chen, which always makes a noun neuter
exercises.explanation: The diminutive suffix -chen always makes a noun grammatically neuter, overriding natural gender. Das Mädchen, das Häuschen, das Büchlein.

exercises.prompt: Match each noun to its article:
exercises.explanation: die Frau, der Mann, das Kind, die Bücher (plural always uses die).

exercises.prompt: Which article is always used for plural nouns?
exercises.explanation: The plural article is always 'die', no matter what the singular gender is. die Tische, die Lampen, die Bücher.

exercises.prompt: ___ Haus (house) — choose the correct article
exercises.explanation: 'das Haus' — house is neuter in German. Das Haus, die Häuser (plural).

sections.title: Tips for Guessing Gender
sections.explanation: While gender must often be memorized, there are helpful patterns. Masculine (-er, -ig, -ling, -ismus, -or): der Lehrer (teacher), der König (king), der Frühling (spring). Feminine (-ung, -heit, -keit, -schaft, -tion, -e): die Wohnung (apartment), die Freiheit (freedom), die Freundschaft (friendship). Neuter (-chen, -lein, -ment, -tum, -um): das Mädchen (girl), das Instrument, das Zentrum. Also: most metals are neuter (das Gold, das Silber), and most infinitives used as nouns are neuter (das Laufen = running).
sections.tip: Key patterns: -ung, -heit, -keit, -schaft → die (feminine). -chen, -lein → das (neuter). -er (agent nouns) → der (masculine).

exercises.prompt: What gender is 'Zeitung' (newspaper)?
exercises.explanation: Words ending in -ung are always feminine: die Zeitung, die Meinung (opinion), die Wohnung (apartment).

exercises.prompt: What gender is 'Freiheit' (freedom)?
exercises.explanation: Words ending in -heit are always feminine: die Freiheit, die Schönheit (beauty), die Gesundheit (health).

exercises.prompt: Nouns ending in -schaft are always ___ gender
exercises.explanation: -schaft nouns are always feminine (die): die Freundschaft, die Gesellschaft (society), die Mannschaft (team).

exercises.prompt: What gender is 'Büchlein' (little book)?
exercises.explanation: Diminutive suffixes -lein (and -chen) always produce neuter nouns: das Büchlein, das Häuschen.

exercises.prompt: Match each suffix to its gender:
exercises.explanation: -ung, -heit, -keit, -schaft = feminine. -chen, -lein, -ment, -tum = neuter.

exercises.prompt: What gender is 'Freundschaft' (friendship)?
exercises.explanation: -schaft words are always feminine: die Freundschaft, die Gesellschaft, die Mannschaft.

exercises.prompt: die Schönheit — the suffix -heit tells us this noun is ___
exercises.explanation: -heit always signals a feminine noun. die Schönheit (beauty), die Wahrheit (truth), die Gesundheit (health).

exercises.prompt: Which noun is definitely neuter based on its suffix?
exercises.explanation: -ment nouns are neuter: das Instrument, das Fundament, das Dokument. -ung is feminine, -ling is masculine.

sections.title: Forming Plurals
sections.explanation: German plural formation is less predictable than English — there are multiple patterns. Common patterns: add -e (der Tisch → die Tische), add -er with Umlaut (das Buch → die Bücher), add -en or -n (die Frau → die Frauen), add -s (das Auto → die Autos, often for foreign words), or no change with Umlaut (der Vater → die Väter). Feminine nouns in -ung, -heit, -keit always add -en: die Wohnungen, die Freiheiten. The best approach is to learn the plural form alongside the noun.
sections.tip: Learn nouns as a trio: article + noun + plural. E.g., 'der Tisch / die Tische', 'das Buch / die Bücher'.

exercises.prompt: What is the plural of 'das Buch' (book)?
exercises.explanation: 'das Buch' pluralizes to 'die Bücher' — the vowel u gets an Umlaut (ü) and -er is added.

exercises.prompt: What is the plural of 'die Frau' (woman)?
exercises.explanation: 'die Frau' pluralizes to 'die Frauen' by adding -en.

exercises.prompt: The plural of 'das Auto' (car) is die Autos. The plural suffix used is ___
exercises.explanation: Many loanwords (often from English or French) form their plural by simply adding -s: Autos, Hotels, Computers.

exercises.prompt: What is the plural of 'die Wohnung' (apartment)?
exercises.explanation: Nouns ending in -ung always form their plural with -en: die Wohnungen.

exercises.prompt: Match each noun to its plural form:
exercises.explanation: German has many plural patterns: Tische (-e), Kinder (-er), Frauen (-en), Autos (-s).

exercises.prompt: What article do ALL plural nouns use?
exercises.explanation: All plural nouns use 'die', regardless of their singular gender: der Mann → die Männer, das Kind → die Kinder, die Frau → die Frauen.

exercises.prompt: der Mann → die ___ (men: add Umlaut + -er ending: Mann → Männer)
exercises.explanation: 'der Mann' (man) pluralizes to 'die Männer'. The 'a' gets an Umlaut (ä) and -er is added.
